# Deception Ladder

Manufactured traction exists on a spectrum. This ladder helps distinguish aggressive but acceptable marketing from deceptive or fraudulent conduct.

| Level | Name | Description | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Product/value improvement | Improve the offer, UX, content, or customer experience | Low |
| 1 | Transparent persuasion | Paid ads, disclosed partnerships, honest positioning | Low/moderate |
| 2 | Aggressive optimization | Strong hooks, repeated testing, fast iteration, platform-native formatting | Moderate if misleading |
| 3 | Context concealment | Hidden sponsorship, unclear incentives, paid UGC without visible disclosure | High |
| 4 | Signal distortion | Fake engagement, fake reviews, account networks, fake installs | Very high |
| 5 | Measurement fraud | Attribution theft, fake leads, bot streams, invalid traffic | Very high/legal |
| 6 | Institutional deception | Inflated ARR, fake compliance, forged endorsements, false identity | Very high/legal |

## Why the ladder matters

Not every uncomfortable tactic is fraud. The paper should avoid moral overreach. The defensible argument is that the ethical problem escalates when actors conceal material context or falsify the evidence used by systems and people.
